section.title: God Is Love
section.sectionRationale: This is the summit of the letter. John grounds the call to love one another in the very being of God: God is love, shown supremely in sending His Son as the atoning sacrifice for our sins. Because He first loved us, we love, and His love perfected in us casts out fear and gives confidence for the day of judgment.
section.thesis: God is love, revealed in sending His one and only Son as the atoning sacrifice for our sins, so that those who know this love are born of God and love one another, and this perfected love drives out fear and gives confidence before the God who first loved us.
section.frameNote: Four beats: love comes from God, for God is love shown in the cross; God abides in those who love; love perfected gives confidence and casts out fear; and we love because He first loved us, so love for God and brother are one.

units.label: God is love, shown in the cross
units.note: the atoning sacrifice for our sins

verses.text: Beloved, let us {{agape|love}} one another, because love comes from God. Everyone who loves has been born of God and knows God.
Whoever does not love does not know God, because God is love.
This is how God's love was revealed among us: God sent His {{monogenes|one and only}} Son into the world, so that we might live through Him.
And love consists in this: not that we loved God, but that He loved us and sent His Son as the {{hilasmos|atoning sacrifice}} for our sins.

units.label: God abides in those who love

verses.text: Beloved, if God so loved us, we also ought to love one another.
No one has ever seen God; but if we love one another, God remains in us, and His love is {{teleioo|perfected}} in us.
By this we know that we remain in Him, and He in us: He has given us of His Spirit.
And we have seen and testify that the Father has sent His Son to be the Savior of the world.
If anyone confesses that Jesus is the Son of God, God abides in him, and he in God.

units.label: Love casts out fear
units.note: confidence on the day of judgment

verses.text: And we have come to know and believe the love that God has for us. God is love; whoever {{meno|abides}} in love abides in God, and God in him.
In this way, love has been perfected among us, so that we may have confidence on the day of judgment; for in this world we are just like Him.
There is no {{phobos|fear}} in love, but perfect love drives out fear, because fear involves punishment. The one who fears has not been perfected in love.

units.label: We love because He first loved us
units.note: loving God and brother as one

verses.text: We love because He first loved us.
If anyone says, “I love God,” but hates his brother, he is a liar. For anyone who does not love his brother, whom he has seen, cannot love God, whom he has not seen.
And we have this commandment from Him: Whoever loves God must love his brother as well.

agape.gloss: Love, self-giving love that seeks another's good at cost to oneself. It comes from God and reflects His very nature, for God is love. This is the love displayed at the cross and poured into believers, who love one another because they have first been loved.

monogenes.gloss: One and only, unique, only-begotten. It marks Jesus as the Father's one and only Son, utterly unique in His relation to the Father. That God sent this Son, and not a lesser gift, into the world shows the immeasurable costliness of His love.

hilasmos.gloss: Atoning sacrifice, propitiation, the offering that both covers sin and turns away wrath. God's love is defined not by sentiment but by sending His Son as the hilasmos for our sins, so that at the cross His justice and His love meet and the way to life is opened.

teleioo.gloss: To complete, bring to its goal, make perfect in the sense of mature and fully realised. God's love reaches its intended end when it is received and then expressed in love for one another, and it gives confidence for the day of judgment. It is love coming to full flower, not sinless flawlessness.

meno.gloss: To remain, abide, dwell, continue. John's key word for mutual indwelling: whoever abides in love abides in God, and God in him. To live in love is to live in God, and it is the sphere in which the believer and God share an ongoing, settled communion.

phobos.gloss: Fear, dread, terror. The fear that has to do with punishment, the cringing dread of judgment, is driven out by perfect love. This is not the reverent awe Scripture commends but the servile terror of condemnation, which cannot coexist with the assurance of being loved by God.

basic.context: This is the high point of the letter. Why should we love one another? Because love comes from God, and God is love. And John shows exactly what that love is: not a vague feeling, but God sending His only Son to be the atoning sacrifice for our sins. That is the definition of love. Since God loved us like that, we must love each other. When we do, God lives in us and His love reaches its goal in us, giving us such confidence that fear of judgment is driven out. We love because He first loved us.
basic.hermeneutics: God is love is one of the most quoted and most twisted lines in the Bible. John does not mean love is God, or that God is only softness. He anchors love in the cross, the propitiation for sins, so that God's love is holy, costly, and self-giving. The passage also ties assurance to love: love perfected casts out the fear of judgment, not because we are flawless, but because we rest in the God who loved us first and made us like His Son.
basic.originalLanguages: God is love, agape, revealed in sending His one and only, monogenes, Son as the atoning sacrifice, hilasmos, for our sins. When we love, His love is perfected, teleioo, brought to its goal, in us. Whoever abides, meno, in love abides in God, and such perfected love drives out fear, phobos, the dread of punishment.
basic.typology: God sending His one and only Son as the atoning sacrifice fulfils the whole sacrificial system and echoes the offering of the beloved son foreshadowed in Isaac. The love that casts out the fear of judgment answers the terror of a holy God that ran through the old covenant, now stilled in Christ.
basic.christ: Christ is the definition and demonstration of love: the one and only Son sent as the propitiation for our sins so that we might live through Him. Confessing Him as the Son of God is where God comes to abide in us, and being made like Him is the ground of confidence on the day of judgment.
basic.god: God is love, not as a slogan but as His very nature, proven at the cross. He is also just, which is why love takes the form of a propitiation. He loved first, before we loved Him, and He gives His Spirit and His Son so that His love may reach its goal in a people who love one another.
basic.crossReferences: John 3:16 says God so loved the world that He gave His one and only Son. Romans 5:8 says God shows His love in that Christ died for us while we were sinners. Romans 8 gives confidence that nothing separates us from God's love.
basic.oneStory: The whole Bible has been unveiling the love of a holy God for undeserving people. Here it reaches its fullest statement: God is love, and He proved it by giving His only Son as the atoning sacrifice. That love, received and returned in love for one another, casts out fear and forms the family of God, the goal toward which the entire story has moved.

context.literary: The theological heart of the letter, weaving together the themes of love, new birth, knowing God, abiding, the sending of the Son, the Spirit, and assurance. It circles repeatedly around 'God is love', deepening each time, and joins doctrine, God's love in the cross, to ethics, our love for one another.
context.historical: Against teachers who prized knowledge of God while failing to love the believers they left, John insists that no one who fails to love truly knows God, for God is love. The costly love of the cross rebukes any spirituality that is high on claims and empty of self-giving.
context.canonical: The passage crowns the biblical revelation of God's love, joining John 3:16 and Romans 5:8, and it grounds Christian ethics in the character and act of God. Its teaching that God is love must be read with the whole canon's witness to God's holiness and justice, held together at the cross.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To ground the command to love one another in the nature and act of God, who is love and who sent His Son as the atoning sacrifice, and to assure believers that receiving and returning this love brings God's indwelling, casts out fear, and gives confidence for the day of judgment.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage both describes God, God is love, and His act, He sent His Son, and prescribes the response, let us love one another. The assurances, God abides in us, perfect love casts out fear, describe the settled experience of those who rest in and reflect God's love.

debates.question: What does 'God is love' mean, and what does it not mean?

views.label: Love is essential to God's nature, defined by the cross and held with His holiness
views.case: John says God is love and immediately defines it by the sending of the Son as the propitiation for sins, so love is central to who God is, yet it is holy, costly, and self-giving, not mere sentiment. Read with the letter's insistence that God is light, 1 John 1:5, and just, this means God's love and holiness are not in tension but meet at the cross. This is the strongest orthodox reading.

views.label: God is only love, and nothing else about Him matters
views.case: Some make love the sole attribute, so that holiness, justice, and wrath are denied; but the same letter says God is light and presents Christ as the propitiation that turns away wrath.

views.label: 'God is love' means love itself is divine
views.case: Others invert it to 'love is God', sanctifying any strong affection as divine; but John says God is love, defined by the cross, not that every human love is God.

debates.bearing: The strongest reading holds that love is essential to God's very being, supremely displayed in giving His Son as the atoning sacrifice, and that this love is inseparable from His holiness and justice. This guards against both a sentimental god with no holiness and a cold god with no love, keeping the cross at the centre.

debates.question: How does 'perfect love drives out fear' relate to the fear of God the Bible commends?

views.label: It removes the servile dread of punishment, not reverent awe
views.case: John specifies that the fear cast out 'involves punishment', the cringing terror of condemnation on the day of judgment. Perfect love gives the believer confidence before God because Christ is the propitiation and we are made like Him. This is different from the reverent, worshipful fear of the Lord that Scripture commends. This distinction is the strongest reading and fits the context of confidence at judgment.

views.label: It abolishes all fear of God whatsoever
views.case: Some take it to mean no fear of God of any kind remains; but Scripture elsewhere commends the fear of the Lord, so John must mean the specific dread of punishment.

views.label: It means believers should feel no anxiety about anything
views.case: Others generalise it to freedom from all anxiety; but John's point is narrower, the fear of judgment removed by assurance in God's love.

debates.bearing: The strongest orthodox reading is that perfect love casts out the servile fear of punishment and condemnation, giving confidence before God grounded in Christ's atoning work, while leaving intact the reverent awe of God that Scripture calls the beginning of wisdom. Assurance and reverence stand together.

study.originalLanguages: John lifts the call to love to its highest ground: God is agape, love, in His very being. That love is not sentiment but was revealed when God sent His monogenes, His one and only Son, as the hilasmos, the atoning sacrifice that covers sin and turns away wrath. Received and returned, this love is teleioo, brought to its goal in us, so that whoever lives, meno, abides in love, abides in God and God in him. And where that love has come to its full flower, there is confidence at judgment, for perfect love drives out phobos, the cringing fear that has to do with punishment. The order is everything: we love because He first loved us.

typology.type: God sending His one and only Son as the atoning sacrifice
typology.fulfillment: The whole sacrificial system and the offering of the beloved son foreshadowed in Isaac, Genesis 22, fulfilled in the Father giving His only Son as the propitiation for our sins.

typology.type: Perfect love casting out the fear of judgment
typology.fulfillment: The terror of standing before a holy God under the old covenant, stilled for those in Christ who are made like Him and await the day of judgment with confidence.

study.christ: Christ is the measure and the means of the love that John celebrates. God's love is defined by the sending of the one and only Son as the propitiation for our sins, so that we might live through Him. To confess Jesus as the Son of God is the point where God comes to abide in us, and because in this world we are being made like Him, we can face the day of judgment with confidence rather than dread. The cross is where God is love is proven and where our fear is answered.
study.god: God is love in His essential being, and He is love that gives, not merely feels: He sent His only Son as the atoning sacrifice while we were still unloving. This same God is light and just, so His love takes the costly form of propitiation rather than the cheap form of indulgence. He loved first, before we loved Him, and He gives His Spirit and His Son so that His love may reach its goal in a people transformed to love one another and freed from the fear of judgment.

crossReferences.note: God so loved the world that He gave His one and only Son, the sending John points to as the revelation of God's love.
God shows His love in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us, matching love defined by the cross, not our worthiness.
Nothing can separate us from the love of God in Christ, the ground of the confidence that casts out fear.
Jesus is the atoning sacrifice for our sins, the same propitiation that here defines the love of God.

study.oneStory: The whole of Scripture has been unveiling the love of a holy God for people who did not deserve it. Here that revelation reaches its fullest word: God is love, and He proved it by giving His one and only Son as the atoning sacrifice for our sins. Received and returned in love for one another, this love casts out fear and forms the family of God. The God who is love has been drawing His people to Himself from the beginning, and at the cross the story's deepest truth stands revealed.

sermon.title: Let Us Love One Another
sermon.why: Begg preaches this section, 1 John 4:7-12, on love that comes from God, God's love revealed in sending His Son as the atoning sacrifice, and love perfected in us. A clear, orthodox handling of the letter's summit.

quiz.q: How does John define God's love in verses 9 and 10?

options.text: God sent His one and only Son as the atoning sacrifice for our sins.
options.note: Love is defined by the cross, God's costly gift of His Son, not by sentiment.

options.text: God feels warmly toward everyone regardless of the cross.
options.note: John grounds love in the sending of the Son as the propitiation, not in feeling alone.

options.text: God rewards those who love Him first.
options.note: John says the opposite: not that we loved God, but that He loved us first.

options.text: God gives good gifts in this life.
options.note: God does give good gifts, but the defining act of love here is sending His Son as the atoning sacrifice.

quiz.q: What does 'God is love' mean, and not mean?

options.text: Love is essential to God's nature, shown at the cross and held together with His holiness.
options.note: God is love and also light; the two meet in the propitiation, not in tension.

options.text: God is only love, so holiness and justice do not matter.
options.note: Love truly is essential to God, but the same letter says God is light and presents Christ as the propitiation, so holiness and justice are not set aside.

options.text: Love itself is God, so any strong affection is divine.
options.note: John says God is love defined by the cross, not that every human love is God.

options.text: God is distant and His love is only an idea.
options.note: God's love is concrete and active, proven by sending His Son into the world.

quiz.q: According to verse 19, why do believers love?

options.text: Because He first loved us.
options.note: Our love is always a response to God's prior love, never its cause.

options.text: Because loving earns God's love in return.
options.note: God loved us first; our love does not purchase His.

options.text: Because it feels good to be loving.
options.note: The root is God's prior love for us, not the pleasant feeling of loving.

options.text: Because others love us and we return it.
options.note: Human love matters, but John grounds our love in God loving us first.

quiz.q: What does 'perfect love drives out fear' mean (verse 18)?

options.text: It removes the servile dread of punishment, giving confidence before God at judgment.
options.note: The fear cast out involves punishment; reverent awe of God remains.

options.text: It abolishes every kind of fear of God, including reverent awe.
options.note: Love truly drives out the dread of judgment, but Scripture commends the reverent fear of the Lord, which remains.

options.text: It guarantees believers will never feel any anxiety about anything.
options.note: John's point is narrower: the fear of judgment removed by assurance in God's love.

options.text: It means judgment is not real for anyone.
options.note: John speaks of confidence on the day of judgment, which he treats as real.

quiz.q: What does John say about claiming to love God while hating a brother?

options.text: Such a person is a liar; you cannot love the God you have not seen while hating the brother you have.
options.note: Verse 20 exposes love for God that ignores the brother as false.

options.text: It is acceptable as long as your feelings toward God are sincere.
options.note: John calls it a lie; love for God and brother cannot be separated.

options.text: Loving God and loving people are entirely unrelated.
options.note: John binds them together; the command is to love God and brother alike.

options.text: Loving God is what matters; loving people is optional.
options.note: John makes love for the brother the test of love for God, not an optional extra.

questions: John defines God's love by the cross, not by feeling. How does anchoring love in the sending of the Son as the atoning sacrifice reshape what you mean when you say God loves you?
We love because He first loved us. Where do you tend to reverse the order, trying to earn love before you rest in being loved?
Perfect love casts out the fear of punishment. What fears about God or judgment still linger in you, and how does His love in Christ speak to them?
John says you cannot love God while hating a brother you can see. Is there someone whose face tests the reality of your love for God right now?
God abides in those who love one another. How have you experienced God's presence become more real as you have given yourself in love to others?

translationNotes.note: 'One and only' renders monogenes, unique or only-begotten, as the BSB footnote notes; it marks the singular, costly nature of the Son the Father sent.
'Atoning sacrifice' renders hilasmos, which the BSB footnote gives as 'propitiation'; it carries both the covering of sin and the turning away of God's wrath, defining love by the cross.
'We love' follows a shorter reading; the BSB footnote notes some texts read 'We love Him'. Either way our love is a response to God's prior love.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 John 4:7-21. No em dashes in this passage. Footnotes at 4:9, 4:10, and 4:19 noted per the BSB.
